title: Critical profile
principles: Critical chance and critical multiplier must be evaluated together as a combined damage profile.
Critical chance determines how often an eligible damage instance critically hits, while critical multiplier determines the value gained from that critical hit.
A weapon should only be described as naturally critical-oriented when both critical frequency and critical reward provide meaningful evidence.
Below 100 percent critical chance, the weapon alternates between normal and critical hits, so a high multiplier paired with low chance produces valuable but inconsistent critical events.
At 100 percent critical chance, every eligible damage instance is guaranteed to critically hit.
Critical chance above 100 percent is not wasted; each complete 100 percent interval guarantees another critical tier and the remainder determines the chance of reaching the next tier.
Higher critical tiers continue to benefit from critical multiplier, so multiplier remains relevant after critical chance exceeds 100 percent.

Expected critical contribution can be represented conceptually by the average multiplier relationship, but the final response should not perform arithmetic unless the application explicitly supplies a derived value.
Average critical contribution across many hits does not describe short-term consistency.
High critical chance with a weak multiplier indicates reliable critical activation but limited reward per critical tier.
High critical multiplier with weak critical chance indicates critical potential rather than dependable critical performance.

Critical chance and critical multiplier describe the quality of the critical profile per eligible damage instance; they do not establish total combat output without application frequency and attack behavior.
Use only supplied statistics and never infer critical guarantees, conditional bonuses, external buffs, or hidden mechanics from the weapon name.

aligned: Critical chance and critical multiplier reinforce one another.
The weapon has evidence of recurring critical activation and meaningful reward.
moderate: The critical relationship is usable but not dominant from the supplied values alone.
Critical contribution may support the weapon without defining its complete role.
mixed: One critical statistic is substantially more favorable than the other.
The profile may lack either consistency or reward.
limited: The supplied critical relationship provides limited evidence for a critical-centered role.

exceptions: Do not infer final damage output from critical statistics without application frequency.
Do not assume secondary, radial, chained, or special events share the same critical statistics.
Do not perform numerical critical calculations unless the application supplies the derived result.
